one advertises reachability, the route to the network with source one, it will add a
BGP extended community that specifies relay one and likewise relay two will do the same.
And those routes will, BGP routes will propagate throughout the network. And now these routers all
know which direction to send their joins or their AMT discovery messages.
So the joins for source one get sent to relay one and source two gets sent to relay two.
And the way we learned it was through this BGP extended community. And once we have the join set up,
this is the direction of the traffic flow. Purple is native multi-cast flowing and blue is tunneling
either AMT or pinlight. So what treaty end did was these tunnels, these last mile tunnels from
the relays to the receivers, what this proposal is adding is the ability to do middle-mile tunneling
from a gateway and one island to a relay and another. So router to router tunnels using AMT.
What is the implications of this proposal? Well, this is a really flexible architecture, flexible
dynamic architecture that allows core routers to become AMT gateways.
There is nothing in the AMT spec that prevented routers from becoming gateways. The issue was,
how do they know which relay to use? It's not obvious how they would do that in a world where
you have more than one AMT relay. Another implication is that routers can be both AMT gateways and
relays at the same time. They can be a relay to downstream gateways and they can be a gateway to upstream
relays. So they could build tunnels in both directions and send and receive multi-cast traffic
through those tunnels. This essentially extends the treaty and architecture to support middle-mile
tunneling because really previously, treaty end really only covered the last mile aspect of tunneling
to address receivers on unicast only islands on unicast only networks as opposed to what if the
source was on a separate island than where the receivers are. Why would we want to do this?
